article_meta.title: A California judge scolded members of Mark Zuckerberg's legal team after they showed up to a Los Angeles courtroom weari

transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective included

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: A California judge scolded members of Mark Zuckerberg's legal team after they showed up to a Los Angeles courtroom weari
SOURCE: Newsmax (News)

ARTICLE:

A California judge scolded members of Mark Zuckerberg's legal team after they showed up to a Los Angeles courtroom wearing Meta Ray-Ban glasses — camera-equipped smart eyewear — during a high-stakes trial over social media's impact on children. \"The judge upbraided the Meta team and said, 'If you guys have recorded anything, you have to dispose of it, or I will hold you in contempt,'\" Jacob Ward, a technology journalist and host of \"The Rip Current\" podcast, told CBS News on Wednesday. Ward described the episode as \"an extraordinary misstep\" by Meta. It remains unclear whether members of Zuckerberg's team were actively using the devices inside the courtroom or how long the glasses were worn. Recording devices and cameras are generally prohibited in Los Angeles County Superior Court, where strict rules govern courtroom conduct. \"Judicial officers have the discretion to place limitations on video recording and photography in their courtroom,\" a spokesperson for the Superior Court of Los Angeles County told CBS News, citing local and state rules. Judge Carolyn Kuhl, who is presiding over the case, ordered anyone wearing artificial intelligence-powered smart glasses in the courtroom to remove them immediately. She also warned that any attempt to use facial recognition technology to identify jurors would not be tolerated. \"This is very serious,\" she reportedly said. Meta Ray-Ban smart glasses retail for between $299 and $799 and are capable of taking photos and recording video — raising concerns about privacy and courtroom security. Zuckerberg appeared in court as part of a closely watched trial examining whether Meta and Alphabet-owned YouTube intentionally designed their social media platforms to drive compulsive use among minors. The plaintiff, identified only by the initials \"KGM,\" alleges that early exposure to social media led to addiction and damaged her mental health — claims that strike at the heart of the national debate over Big Tech's influence on America's youth. Newsmax reached out to Meta for comment but did not receive an immediate response. Nicole Weatherholtz ✉ Nicole Weatherholtz, a Newsmax general assignment reporter covers news, politics, and culture. She is a National Newspaper Association award-winning journalist.
---

{
  "summary": "During a high-profile Los Angeles trial concerning the alleged harmful effects of social media on children, a California judge reprimanded members of Mark Zuckerberg's legal team for wearing Meta Ray-Ban smart glasses in the courtroom. These AI-enabled glasses are capable of recording video and taking photos, raising significant concerns about privacy and courtroom security. The judge, Carolyn Kuhl, warned the Meta team that any unauthorized recording would result in contempt of court, and ordered immediate removal of all smart glasses. She specifically highlighted that facial recognition technology to identify jurors would not be tolerated. The trial itself is focused on whether Meta and YouTube deliberately designed their platforms to encourage compulsive usage among minors, with the anonymous plaintiff 'KGM' claiming early exposure caused addiction and mental health issues. Stringent rules in Los Angeles County Court prohibit such recording devices, and judicial officers retain broad discretion to enforce conduct and privacy standards. Meta did not respond to Newsmax's request for comment on the incident.",
  "key_facts": [
    "Mark Zuckerberg's legal team wore Meta Ray-Ban smart glasses, capable of recording, in an LA courtroom.",
    "Judge Carolyn Kuhl scolded the team, warning them of contempt if any unauthorized recordings occurred.",
    "All smart glasses were ordered removed and use of facial recognition on jurors was strictly prohibited.",
    "The trial addresses claims that Meta and YouTube foster compulsive use among minors, harming their mental health.",
    "Los Angeles courts have strict rules against recording devices, and Meta has not publicly responded to the incident."
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "Newsmax",
    "CBS News"
  ]
}

parsed_output.summary: During a high-profile Los Angeles trial concerning the alleged harmful effects of social media on children, a California judge reprimanded members of Mark Zuckerberg's legal team for wearing Meta Ray-Ban smart glasses in the courtroom. These AI-enabled glasses are capable of recording video and taking photos, raising significant concerns about privacy and courtroom security. The judge, Carolyn Kuhl, warned the Meta team that any unauthorized recording would result in contempt of court, and ordered immediate removal of all smart glasses. She specifically highlighted that facial recognition technology to identify jurors would not be tolerated. The trial itself is focused on whether Meta and YouTube deliberately designed their platforms to encourage compulsive usage among minors, with the anonymous plaintiff 'KGM' claiming early exposure caused addiction and mental health issues. Stringent rules in Los Angeles County Court prohibit such recording devices, and judicial officers retain broad discretion to enforce conduct and privacy standards. Meta did not respond to Newsmax's request for comment on the incident.
key_facts: Mark Zuckerberg's legal team wore Meta Ray-Ban smart glasses, capable of recording, in an LA courtroom.
Judge Carolyn Kuhl scolded the team, warning them of contempt if any unauthorized recordings occurred.
All smart glasses were ordered removed and use of facial recognition on jurors was strictly prohibited.
The trial addresses claims that Meta and YouTube foster compulsive use among minors, harming their mental health.
Los Angeles courts have strict rules against recording devices, and Meta has not publicly responded to the incident.
